description: The provided image displays a generic software-interface element labeled Check for updates with a circular icon containing a red bookmark symbol. It lacks any scientific data, material micrographs, diffraction patterns, spectroscopic peaks, or mechanical curves relevant to low-alloy steel recrystallization. The caption is empty and no citation sentences are provided. Although the accompanying text extraction summary details multi-stage dynamic recrystallization under constant and transient hot-compression conditions—including flow-stress responses, DRX fraction evolutions, EBSD-reconstructed prior-austenite grain sizes, and 3D cellular-automaton predictions for samples deformed at 850–1150 °C and strain rates of 0.01–10 s⁻¹—the visual content cannot be linked to any specific figure, sample ID, or experimental result reported therein. Consequently, no material system, processing condition, phase constituent, grain-size trend, or performance metric can be visually verified or inferred from the image itself, and the figure’s role in supporting the paper’s core conclusions remains undetermined.

The figure presents the complete experimental workflow and varying deformation condition schemes for single-pass hot compression tests on a low-alloy steel using a Gleeble 1500 thermomechanical simulator. Panel (a) illustrates the process route: cylindrical specimens (φ6 mm × 9 mm) are heated to 1250 °C at 10 °C/s, held for 180 s to homogenize austenite, cooled to the target temperature at 10 °C/s, held for 60 s, compressed to a true strain of 0.9 under either constant conditions (850–1150 °C, 0.01–10 s⁻¹) or varying conditions (950–1050 °C, 0.01–0.1 s⁻¹), and immediately water-quenched to freeze the high-temperature microstructure. Post-deformation characterization includes optical microscopy (Olympus GX71) and EBSD (SU5000 SEM) on the side section. Panel (b) details transient strain-rate variation paths at 950 °C, where the strain rate is step-increased or step-decreased between 0.01 s⁻¹ and 0.1 s⁻¹ at fixed true strains of 0.2 or 0.4 until a total true strain of 0.9. Panel (c) shows continuous temperature variation paths at 0.01 s⁻¹, where the temperature is ramped at 10 °C/s between 950 °C and 1050 °C within the strain ranges of 0.2–0.3 or 0.4–0.5. These schemes establish the experimental framework for quantifying multi-stage dynamic recrystallization under non-steady-state hot deformation, providing validation data for the subsequent 3D cellular automaton modeling.

Figure 2 presents a schematic diagram illustrating the conceptual evolution of true stress versus true strain for low-alloy steel under step changes in the Zener-Hollomon parameter (Z_p) at a specific true strain ε_I. The plot is divided into Stage I (before variation) and Stage II (after variation). Four curves are depicted: a black dashed line for constant Z_p1 (lower value), a purple dashed line for constant Z_p2 (higher value, with Z_p2 > Z_p1), a blue solid line for a sudden decrease from Z_p2 to Z_p1 at ε_I, and a red solid line for a sudden increase from Z_p1 to Z_p2 at ε_I. During Stage I, each curve exhibits work hardening followed by dynamic softening, reaching distinct critical strains (ε_c1 < ε_c2 < ε_I) and peak stresses (σ_p2 for the higher Z_p2 path). In Stage II, decreasing Z_p causes an abrupt stress drop toward a new steady-state stress σ_ss1^(v), whereas increasing Z_p produces a stress jump followed by a renewed peak σ_p2^(v) and eventual steady-state stress σ_ss2. This schematic establishes the theoretical framework for modeling multi-stage dynamic recrystallization and transient flow stress responses under varying hot deformation conditions.

This 3D surface plot (Fig. 3) illustrates the variation of the stress softening coefficient $k_s(\varepsilon+\Delta\varepsilon)$ for low-alloy steel under multi-stage hot deformation as a function of the natural logarithm of the Zener-Hollomon parameter of the second stage, $\ln Z_P^{(II)}(\varepsilon+\Delta\varepsilon)$, and the first-stage dynamic recrystallization (DRX) fraction $\varphi_d^{(I)}$. Four horizontal planes correspond to different levels of $\varphi_d^{(I)}$ ranging from approximately 0.1 at the bottom to 0.9 at the top. The color scale indicates $k_s$ values from ~0.2 (blue) to 1.0 (yellow). The figure reveals that $k_s$ declines with increasing difference in $Z_P$ between the two stages and with rising $\varphi_d^{(I)}$. At low first-stage DRX fractions, $k_s$ remains near unity across the $\ln Z_P$ range, while at higher $\varphi_d^{(I)}$ the coefficient drops substantially under divergent inter-stage deformation conditions. This supports the core finding that the saturation-stress softening effect intensifies with greater disparity in thermomechanical parameters and increased prior DRX development during transient deformation.

Figure 4 presents the experimental and simulated characterization of the initial mixed-grain prior austenite microstructure in low-alloy steel. Panel (a) shows a three-dimensional optical metallography reconstruction displaying grain boundary networks with direction-specific average grain sizes of approximately 127.63 μm (X), 131.59 μm (Y), and 166.00 μm (Z), alongside 100 μm scale bars. Panel (b) presents the corresponding 3D cellular automaton (CA) simulated microstructure, rendered with colored grains and X-direction serial slices, reporting simulated averages of 132.32 μm (X), 126.45 μm (Y), and 159.75 μm (Z). Panels (c) through (e) provide quantitative grain size distributions: (c) the experimental metallographic histogram with a 2D average grain size (AGS) of 141.66 μm, standard deviation (SD) of 54.54 μm, minimum of 10.22 μm, and maximum of 265.20 μm; (d) the CA-simulated 2D distribution (AGS = 140.01 μm, SD = 51.88 μm); and (e) the CA-simulated 3D distribution (AGS = 143.78 μm, SD = 58.25 μm). The close agreement between the experimental and simulated histograms, including the right-skewed mixed-grain distribution, demonstrates that the CA model accurately reproduces the inhomogeneous initial microstructure, establishing a reliable baseline for subsequent dynamic recrystallization simulations.

Fig. 5 presents a comprehensive flowchart of the proposed 3D cellular automaton (CA) model based on inhomogeneous dislocation density for simulating multi-stage dynamic recrystallization (DRX) in low-alloy steel under varying deformation conditions. The framework is initialized with inputs including load conditions (temperatures, strain rates, start/end strains for both stages), initial microstructure descriptors (grain orientation, grain size, dislocation density), and model parameters calibrated from a constitutive model (initial work-hardening rate, saturation stress, DRX activation energy, and others). At each step n, the algorithm checks whether total strain exceeds the first-stage strain εI to update the stress-softening coefficient ks and varying saturation stress via constitutive calibration. It then computes average dislocation density evolution, followed by inhomogeneous dislocation accumulation, strain increments associated with dislocation slipping, and local dislocation density updates. Once the critical dislocation density ρc is reached, inhomogeneous nucleation occurs based on specific rate equations, grain-boundary migrations are calculated, and all cellular state variables are updated. The loop terminates at the end strain and outputs grain orientation distribution and dislocation density distribution. This workflow establishes the computational backbone for quantifying transient DRX microstructure evolution under multi-stage thermomechanical loading.

Figure 6 comprises four panels showing experimental (solid lines) and simulated (dashed lines with open circles) dynamic recrystallization (DRX) fraction evolutions as a function of time for a low-alloy steel under isothermal hot compression. Panels (a)–(d) correspond to deformation temperatures of 850 °C, 950 °C, 1050 °C and 1150 °C, respectively. Within each panel, four colored curves represent strain rates of 0.01 s⁻¹ (red), 0.1 s⁻¹ (blue), 1 s⁻¹ (magenta) and 10 s⁻¹ (olive). At a given temperature, increasing strain rate shifts the DRX curves toward shorter times, indicating accelerated recrystallization kinetics, while higher temperatures also promote earlier and faster DRX completion. The simulated curves closely track the experimental sigmoidal transients across all conditions, including the incubation and rapid growth stages. The overall agreement demonstrates the quantitative predictive capability of the inhomogeneous-dislocation-density 3D cellular automaton model for multi-stage DRX under constant deformation conditions.

Figure 7 presents experimental (solid lines) and simulated (dashed lines with open circles) true stress–strain curves for low-alloy steel under isothermal hot compression at four deformation temperatures: (a) 850 °C, (b) 950 °C, (c) 1050 °C, and (d) 1150 °C. Each panel contains four colored curves corresponding to constant strain rates of 0.01 s⁻¹ (green), 0.1 s⁻¹ (magenta), 1 s⁻¹ (blue), and 10 s⁻¹ (red). The curves show that flow stress increases with decreasing temperature and increasing strain rate. At lower strain rates and higher temperatures, the stress reaches a peak followed by steady-state or slight softening characteristic of dynamic recrystallization, whereas at 10 s⁻¹ the stress rises more continuously, indicating dominant work hardening. The close agreement between experimental and simulated data across all tested conditions validates the accuracy of the inhomogeneous-dislocation-density 3D cellular automaton model and the modified constitutive model for predicting multi-stage recrystallization and hot deformation behavior.

Figure 8 presents experimental and simulated true stress–true strain curves for low-alloy steel during hot compression at 950 °C under transient strain-rate variations. Panels (a) and (b) show strain-rate jumps from 0.01 s⁻¹ to 0.1 s⁻¹ applied at ε_I = 0.2 or 0.4, whereas (c) and (d) show drops from 0.1 s⁻¹ to 0.01 s⁻¹ at the same switching strains. In each panel, solid and open-circle experimental curves are compared against dashed simulated curves from Model 1 ((a), (c)) and Model 2 ((b), (d)), with reference constant-rate curves at 0.01 s⁻¹ and 0.1 s⁻¹ also shown. Experiments reveal that sudden strain-rate increases produce lower second-stage peak and steady-state stresses than the constant 0.1 s⁻¹ condition, with larger ε_I yielding lower stresses, while sudden decreases cause the flow stress to fall below the constant 0.01 s⁻¹ level within roughly 0.04 strain. The figure demonstrates that Model 2, which incorporates the softening coefficient k_s, captures the transient stress drop and steady-state reduction more accurately than Model 1, particularly for the delayed softening after a strain-rate decrease.

This figure presents experimental and simulated true stress–true strain curves for low-alloy steel under transient temperature changes during hot compression at a constant strain rate of 0.01 s⁻¹. Panels (a) and (b) show a temperature drop from 1050 °C to 950 °C with transition strains (εI) of 0.2 or 0.4, whereas panels (c) and (d) show a temperature rise from 950 °C to 1050 °C under the same transition strains. In each panel, solid black curves denote constant-temperature experimental baselines (1050 °C or 950 °C), while colored solid lines represent experimental results under varying temperatures and dashed lines show simulations using Model 1 in (a) and (c) and Model 2 in (b) and (d). Upon cooling, the flow stress increases toward the lower-temperature steady-state level after the transition, whereas heating induces a rapid stress drop. The comparison demonstrates that Model 2 more accurately captures the post-transition stress evolution than Model 1, supporting the improved prediction capability of the modified constitutive framework for multi-stage dynamic recrystallization.

Figure 10 presents force-stroke curves from hot compression tests on low-alloy steel, comparing experimental measurements against finite-element simulations using Model 1 and Model 2 under transient deformation conditions. Panels (a) and (b) show strain-rate jumps at 950 °C (0.01↔0.1 s⁻¹) with transition strains of 0.2 and 0.4, respectively, while panels (c) and (d) show temperature jumps at 0.01 s⁻¹ (1050↔950 °C) at the same transition strains. In each panel, solid lines denote experimental data, dashed lines with stars represent Model 1, and dashed lines with circles represent Model 2. Model 2 consistently tracks the experimental force evolution more accurately than Model 1, particularly capturing the force drop and subsequent rise after abrupt decreases in strain rate or temperature increases. Conversely, Model 1 predicts a slower force decline and overestimates the second-stage force. These results demonstrate that optimizing characteristic stresses and t0.5 in the constitutive model improves load prediction reliability under varying hot-working conditions.

The figure presents simulation results of dislocation density distributions and flow behaviors for low-alloy steel under hot compression at 850 °C, comparing two strain rates: 0.01 s⁻¹ (top) and 1 s⁻¹ (bottom). Each panel overlays experimental (solid blue curves) and simulated (magenta circles) true stress–true strain curves, alongside 3D cellular automaton renderings at ε = 0.45 and ε = 0.9. At 0.01 s⁻¹, flow stress rises to a peak then gradually declines toward steady state; embedded 3D maps show dislocation densities evolving from 1×10¹¹ m⁻² to 2.821×10¹⁴ m⁻² at ε = 0.45 and to 3.872×10¹⁴ m⁻² at ε = 0.9, with preferential high-density regions along grain boundaries and lower densities inside dynamically recrystallized (DRX) grains. At 1 s⁻¹, the stress level is markedly higher; dislocation densities reach 1.087×10¹⁵ m⁻² at ε = 0.45 and 1.198×10¹⁵ m⁻² at ε = 0.9, indicating suppressed DRX nucleation and growth. The figure supports that DRX grains accumulate lower dislocation density than the matrix, causing flow softening, and that higher strain rates exacerbate microstructural heterogeneity by leaving more unrecrystallized matrix on the 3D model surfaces.

This figure presents a comprehensive comparison of dynamic recrystallization (DRX) fraction and average grain size (AGS) between experimental measurements and 3D cellular automaton (CA) simulations for low-alloy steel hot-compressed at 850 °C under two strain rates. The two upper line graphs plot the simulated evolution of AGS (red circles, left axis) and DRX fraction (blue stars, right axis) versus true strain at 0.01 s⁻¹ and 1 s⁻¹, respectively, alongside analytical model predictions (dashed lines). At 0.01 s⁻¹, the 3D CA predicts AGS drops to 34.53 μm and DRX fraction reaches 84.98% at strain 0.9, whereas at 1 s⁻¹ AGS only falls to 65.87 μm with 49.59% DRX. The accompanying experimental EBSD inverse pole figure (IPF) maps show side-section microstructures at 0.9 strain, yielding AGS of 34.01 ± 4.71 μm and 61.38 ± 7.41 μm, respectively. Adjacent DRX distribution maps report DRX fractions of 79.3% and 42.8%, while the 2D CA-simulated YZ-surface microstructures give corresponding values of 32.94 μm (76.4%) and 59.08 μm (38.7%). The results demonstrate that lower strain rates accelerate DRX kinetics and produce finer recrystallized grains, and that the 3D CA model quantitatively reproduces both the microstructural morphology and DRX statistics observed experimentally.

Figure 13 presents the predicted and experimental flow stress evolution of low-alloy steel under transient deformation conditions using 3D cellular automaton (CA) models. The four panels compare true stress–true strain curves (experimental solid lines versus simulated dashed or circle-marked lines) during hot compression involving step changes in strain rate or temperature. Panel (a) at 950°C shows stress transients when the strain rate increases from 0.01 s⁻¹ to 0.1 s⁻¹ at true strains of 0.2 or 0.4, exhibiting lower peak and steady-state stresses than constant 0.1 s⁻¹. Panel (b) at 950°C depicts stress relaxation upon decreasing strain rate from 0.1 s⁻¹ to 0.01 s⁻¹, dropping below the constant 0.01 s⁻¹ level. Panel (c) at 0.01 s⁻¹ captures stress elevation when temperature decreases from 1050°C to 950°C, while panel (d) at 0.01 s⁻¹ shows rapid stress decline after increasing temperature from 950°C to 1050°C. In all cases, the 3D CA predictions closely match the experimental flow behavior, validating the model's capability to quantify multi-stage dynamic recrystallization under varying deformation conditions.

This figure presents simulated DRX fraction versus true strain curves for low-alloy steel under constant and transient deformation conditions, comparing predictions from Model 2 (left column) and 3D cellular automaton (right column). Panels (a) and (b) show strain-rate jumps at 950 °C—0.01 to 0.1 s⁻¹ and 0.1 to 0.01 s⁻¹—with transitions at 0.2 or 0.4 strain. Panels (c) and (d) show temperature transients at 0.01 s⁻¹—1050 to 950 °C and 950 to 1050 °C—at the same switch points. Solid lines denote constant-condition baselines, while dashed lines represent two-stage paths. When Zp increases (a, c), both models exhibit delayed DRX kinetics relative to the higher constant rate, but CA captures the subsequent pseudo-metadynamic growth of DRXGsI and eventual refinement by DRXGsII. When Zp decreases (b, d), CA predicts DRX fractions that surpass corresponding constant-condition values due to rapid grain growth and enhanced DRV, whereas Model 2 cannot exceed the constant baseline. These comparisons demonstrate that the inhomogeneous-dislocation-density 3D CA more accurately quantifies multi-stage recrystallization kinetics and transient softening than the phenomenological constitutive model.

Figure 15 presents Model 1 simulations of dynamic recrystallization (DRX) fraction evolution versus true strain for low-alloy steel under transiently decreasing Zener-Hollomon parameter (Zp) conditions. Panel (a) at 950 °C compares constant strain rates of 0.01 s⁻¹ and 0.1 s⁻¹ (solid curves) against transient decreases from 0.1 to 0.01 s⁻¹ initiated at first-stage strains of 0.2 or 0.4 (dashed curves). Panel (b) at a constant 0.01 s⁻¹ compares isothermal conditions at 950 °C and 1050 °C (solid curves) against transient temperature increases from 950 to 1050 °C at transition strains of 0.2 or 0.4 (dashed curves). In both cases, transient reduction of Zp accelerates DRX kinetics in the second stage relative to the higher-Zp constant condition, with the DRX fraction eventually approaching or exceeding the lower-Zp baseline depending on the transition strain. The figure demonstrates that the first-stage deformation history significantly influences subsequent recrystallization behavior, serving as a benchmark for evaluating Model 1's predictive capability for multi-stage DRX under varying hot deformation conditions.

Figure 16 presents four line graphs comparing experimental measurements (solid circles with error bars) and 3D cellular automaton (CA) simulations (dashed lines) of average grain size (AGS) for low-alloy steel during hot compression under transient conditions. Subplots (a) and (b) show AGS evolution at 950 °C following strain-rate transients between 0.01 and 0.1 s⁻¹ imposed at first-stage strains of 0.2 (red) or 0.4 (blue). Subplots (c) and (d) show AGS at a constant strain rate of 0.01 s⁻¹ with temperature transients between 1050 and 950 °C at the same first-stage strains. In all panels, AGS decreases with true strain as dynamic recrystallization proceeds. For increasing strain rate (a), the later transient at 0.4 strain yields a larger final AGS than at 0.2 strain, whereas for decreasing strain rate (b) both conditions converge to approximately 50 μm. Decreasing temperature (c) produces finer grains than increasing temperature (d), where the AGS stabilizes near 75–80 μm. The close agreement between experimental and simulated curves validates that the inhomogeneous-dislocation-density 3D CA model reliably captures multi-stage dynamic recrystallization and grain refinement under varying deformation parameters.

Figure 17 presents experimental and simulated microstructure evolutions in low-alloy steel during hot compression at 950 °C under constant and transiently increasing strain rates. Panels (a) and (b) show experimental EBSD reconstructed inverse pole figure maps and corresponding GOS maps. At 0.9 strain, constant 0.1 s⁻¹ yields an average grain size of 28.89±3.09 μm with 95.3% DRX fraction, whereas transiently increasing from 0.01 to 0.1 s⁻¹ at ε_I=0.2 and 0.4 produces coarser grains (31.93±2.03 μm and 37.16±2.26 μm, respectively) with similarly high DRX fractions (~94–96%), indicating that delayed strain-rate transitions leave more residual first-stage grains. Panel (b) further compares microstructures at intermediate strains, showing grain refinement from 65.33 μm (0.01 s⁻¹, 0.4 strain) toward 50.53 μm (0.1 s⁻¹, 0.5 strain). Panels (c), (d), and (e) display 3D cellular automaton simulations: (c) under constant 0.1 s⁻¹, grain size evolves from 119.27 μm at 0.3 strain (13.0% DRX) to 31.07 μm at 0.9 strain (98.7% DRX); (d) for a strain-rate jump at ε_I=0.2, DRX progresses slowly to 98.2% by 0.9 strain (33.77 μm); and (e) for a jump at ε_I=0.4, 76.9% DRX at 0.4 strain rises to 97.9% at 0.9 strain (37.12 μm), with white, green, and red grains denoting matrix, first-stage, and second-stage grains, respectively. The results collectively validate that the inhomogeneous-dislocation-density 3D CA model captures multi-stage DRX evolution and that transient strain-rate increases promote heterogeneous grain structures with larger final average grain sizes than constant high strain-rate conditions.

Figure 18 presents two grouped bar charts comparing experimental and 3D cellular automaton (CA) simulated dynamic recrystallization (DRX) fraction evolutions in low-alloy steel after transient deformation changes imposed at a true strain of 0.4. Panel (a) addresses varying strain rate at 950 °C: the upper group shows DRX fractions after increasing strain rate (0.01 → 0.1 s⁻¹), where both experiment and simulation rise progressively from 0.757/0.769 at 0.4 strain to 0.958/0.979 at 0.9 strain; the lower group shows decreasing strain rate (0.1 → 0.01 s⁻¹), characterized by initially low DRX fractions at 0.4 strain (0.395/0.429) followed by a rapid jump to 0.955/0.991 at 0.5 strain and 0.975/0.998 at 0.9 strain, indicating delayed but accelerated recrystallization. Panel (b) addresses varying temperature at 0.01 s⁻¹: the upper group (decreasing temperature, 1050 → 950 °C) shows steady growth from 0.914/0.943 at 0.4 strain to 0.98/0.986 at 0.9 strain; the lower group (increasing temperature, 950 → 1050 °C) shows a marked increase from 0.757/0.769 at 0.4 strain to 0.979/0.995 at 0.5 strain and 0.99/0.998 at 0.9 strain. In all cases, the 3D CA predictions closely match the experimental measurements, supporting the model's reliability for quantifying multi-stage DRX kinetics under transient hot-deformation conditions.

This figure presents experimental and simulated microstructure evolutions in low-alloy steel during hot compression at 0.01 s⁻¹ under constant and decreasing temperatures. Panel (a) compares reconstructed grain orientation maps and corresponding GOS maps at 0.9 strain for a constant temperature of 950 °C versus decreasing temperature from 1050 °C to 950 °C with first-stage strains (ε_I) of 0.2 and 0.4, showing DRX fractions around 96–97 % but larger grain sizes for the decreasing-temperature path (60.93 ± 2.91 μm at ε_I = 0.4) compared to the constant condition (52.01 ± 3.48 μm). Panel (b) displays reconstructed microstructures at intermediate strains (0.4–0.5), revealing transient microstructural states during the temperature drop. Panels (c) and (d) show 3D cellular automaton simulations: (c) at constant 950 °C where average grain size decreases from 68.69 μm at 0.4 strain to 52.52 μm at 0.9 strain and DRX fraction rises from 76.9 % to 99.5 %; and (d) for decreasing temperature in the strain range 0.4–0.5, where average grain size reduces from 81.12 μm to 58.30 μm and DRX fraction increases from 76.9 % to 98.6 %, with colors distinguishing matrix (white), first-stage (green, 1050 °C), and second-stage (red, 950 °C) grains. The figure supports that multi-stage recrystallization under transient thermal paths yields coarser final grains than constant-temperature deformation despite comparable DRX fractions, validating the 3D CA model for quantifying multi-stage DRX behavior.

Figure 20 presents the experimental and simulated microstructure evolutions of low-alloy steel during hot deformation at 950 °C with a transient strain-rate decrease from 0.1 s⁻¹ to 0.01 s⁻¹ at a true strain of 0.4. Panel (a) shows EBSD inverse pole figure (IPF) maps (top row) and grain orientation spread (GOS) maps (bottom row) at three stages: after the first stage at 0.4 strain (left, average grain size 81.50±6.32 μm, DRX fraction 38.9%), at 0.5 strain following the rate drop (middle, 52.92±3.35 μm, DRX fraction 95.2%), and at 0.9 strain (right, 50.71±3.29 μm, DRX fraction 97.7%). Panel (b) displays the corresponding 3D cellular automaton simulation results, where white grains represent the matrix, green grains denote the first stage (950 °C, 0.1 s⁻¹), and red grains denote the second stage (950 °C, 0.01 s⁻¹). The simulated DRX fractions increase from 42.9% at 0.4 strain to 99.1% at 0.5 strain and 99.8% at 0.9 strain, with average grain sizes of 82.68 μm, 50.78 μm, and 52.58 μm, respectively. The figure demonstrates that decreasing the strain rate triggers rapid dynamic recrystallization and grain refinement, and that the 3D CA model successfully captures this multi-stage recrystallization behavior.

Figure 21 presents a comparative analysis of experimental and simulated microstructure evolution in low-alloy steel under constant and increasing-temperature deformation at 0.01 s⁻¹. Panels (a) and (b) display reconstructed experimental EBSD grain-orientation maps (upper rows) and corresponding GOS maps (lower rows) at true strains of 0.5 and 0.9, respectively. Under constant 1050 °C, the average grain size (AGS) is 80.01 ± 4.30 μm at 0.5 strain with a DRX fraction of 98.6%, increasing slightly to 81.30 ± 4.39 μm at 0.9 strain. In contrast, the increasing-temperature path (950 to 1050 °C in the strain range 0.4–0.5) yields a finer microstructure at 0.5 strain (62.63 ± 4.11 μm, DRX 97.3%) that coarsens to 79.69 ± 4.78 μm by 0.9 strain. Panels (c) and (d) show 3D cellular-automaton simulations for the constant and varying-temperature conditions, respectively. The constant-temperature simulation predicts AGS values of 79.79 μm (0.5 strain, 98.2% DRX) and 79.89 μm (0.9 strain, 98.6% DRX), while the increasing-temperature simulation gives 65.61 μm (0.5 strain, 99.5% DRX) and 75.90 μm (0.9 strain, 99.8% DRX), with green and red grains distinguishing the first (950 °C) and second (1050 °C) DRX stages. These results demonstrate that transient heating refines grains at intermediate strains but subsequent coarsening occurs, and the inhomogeneous-dislocation-density 3D CA model quantitatively captures the multi-stage DRX evolution.

Figure 22 presents simulated evolutions of dynamic recrystallization (DRX) fraction and average dislocation density in low-alloy steel under transient deformation conditions, predicted by 3D cellular automaton models incorporating inhomogeneous dislocation density. Subplots (a) and (b) plot DRX fraction against true strain when transiently increasing the Zener-Hollomon parameter Z_P: (a) shows a strain-rate increase from 0.01 s⁻¹ to 0.1 s⁻¹ at 950 °C initiated at first-stage strains of 0.2 or 0.4, while (b) shows a temperature decrease from 1050 °C to 950 °C at 0.01 s⁻¹ within strain ranges of 0.2–0.3 or 0.4–0.5. In both, the total DRX fraction (solid lines), first-stage DRXGs_I (dashed), and second-stage DRXGs_II (dash-dotted) reveal that the second-stage fraction begins to rise only after a delay, during which total DRX growth relies predominantly on DRXGs_I, and the first-stage fraction subsequently declines as the second-stage fraction increases. Subplots (c) and (d) show the corresponding average dislocation densities of matrix grains, DRXGs_I, and DRXGs_II under identical conditions; the DRXGs_II curves consistently lie below both the matrix and DRXGs_I curves. Citation text explains that the lower dislocation density in DRXGs_II provides the driving force to refine both the matrix and the first-stage DRX grains, and that the delayed DRXGs_II nucleation stems from an elevated critical dislocation density caused by the Z_P jump. Together, these curves demonstrate that transient hardening conditions induce distinct two-stage DRX kinetics and inhomogeneous dislocation partitioning, which govern subsequent microstructural refinement.

This figure presents simulated evolutions of microstructure and dislocation density distribution on the YZ surface of low-alloy steel under transient deformation conditions using a 3D cellular automaton model. Panels (a) and (b) illustrate the effects of transiently increasing strain rate from 0.01 s⁻¹ to 0.1 s⁻¹ at 0.2 and 0.4 strain under 950 °C, respectively, while panel (c) shows decreasing temperature from 1050 °C to 950 °C during the strain range of 0.4–0.5 at 0.01 s⁻¹. Each row contains paired microstructure maps—where white, green, and red grains represent the matrix, first-stage DRX, and second-stage DRX—and corresponding dislocation density maps with respective color scales. Quantified DRX fractions indicate that second-stage DRX grains progressively nucleate and consume earlier grains. As stated in the accompanying text, the lower dislocation density in DRX_GSII compared to both the matrix and DRX_GSI provides the driving force for grain refinement and stress softening during multi-stage recrystallization.

This figure presents simulated evolutions of dynamic recrystallization (DRX) fraction and average dislocation density in low-alloy steel under transiently decreasing Zener-Hollomon parameter (Z_P) using 3D cellular automaton (CA) models with inhomogeneous dislocation density. Subfigures (a) and (b) plot DRX fraction versus true strain, while (c) and (d) plot average dislocation density versus true strain. Panels (a) and (c) show results for a transient strain-rate decrease from 0.1 s⁻¹ to 0.01 s⁻¹ at 950 °C with first-stage strains (ε₁) of 0.2 and 0.4. Panels (b) and (d) show results for a temperature increase from 950 °C to 1050 °C at 0.01 s⁻¹ within strain ranges of 0.2–0.3 or 0.4–0.5. In all cases, curves distinguish total DRX, first-stage DRX grains (DRXGs_I), and second-stage DRX grains (DRXGs_II), plus matrix grains in (c) and (d). The figure reveals that after decreasing Z_P, the DRX fraction of DRXGs_I initially rises rapidly and then declines gradually due to refinement by DRXGs_II. Concurrently, enhanced dynamic recovery (DRV) and DRX grain refinement cause a marked drop in the average dislocation density of both the matrix and DRXGs_I during the initial part of the second stage, explaining the accelerated stress softening relative to constant deformation conditions.

Figure 25 presents simulated evolutions of microstructure and dislocation density distribution on the YZ surface of low-alloy steel; the caption reports a temperature of 950 °C and that panel (a) shows a transiently decreasing strain rate from 0.1 s⁻¹ to 0.01 s⁻¹ at 0.4 true strain, whereas panel (b) shows a constant strain rate of 0.01 s⁻¹ at 0.42 true strain. The left column of (a) displays microstructure maps at 0.4, 0.42, and 0.45 strain, with a visible legend indicating white grains as matrix, green grains as first-stage dynamically recrystallized (DRX) grains, and red grains as second-stage DRX grains. The right column provides corresponding dislocation density distributions with a visible color scale spanning 0.01 to 8 × 10¹³ m⁻². At 0.4 strain, image labels indicate a first-stage DRX fraction of 42.9% and a dislocation density range of 1 × 10¹¹–3.689 × 10¹⁴ m⁻². By 0.42 strain, first-stage DRX is 84.8% with total DRX at 85.8%, and the maximum dislocation density drops to 1.133 × 10¹⁴ m⁻². At 0.45 strain, first-stage DRX reaches 93.3% and total DRX reaches 96.7%, with dislocation density up to 7.167 × 10¹³ m⁻². In (b), the microstructure exhibits a DRX fraction of 83.0% and the dislocation density ranges up to 5.169 × 10¹³ m⁻². These maps demonstrate that a transient strain rate reduction promotes rapid DRX growth and significant dislocation density reduction, with second-stage grains marking multi-stage recrystallization, supporting the core conclusion that transient deformation conditions strongly influence DRX kinetics and spatial heterogeneity.

This figure presents 3D cellular automaton simulated evolutions of microstructure and dislocation density distribution on the YZ surface for low-alloy steel during hot compression at 0.01 s⁻¹, comparing increasing and constant temperature conditions. Panel (a) shows the increasing-temperature path from 950 °C to 1050 °C at strains of 0.2, 0.23, and 0.3. Microstructures are coded as white grains (matrix), green grains (first-stage DRX), and red grains (second-stage DRX). The DRX fraction increases from 3.8% at 0.2 strain to 98.1% at 0.3 strain, with second-stage DRX becoming dominant. Corresponding dislocation density maps reveal high densities in unrecrystallized matrix grains (labeled A, B, C; up to ~1.5×10¹⁴ m⁻²) and significantly lower densities in recrystallized grains. Panel (b) shows the constant 1050 °C condition at 0.23 strain, where the DRX fraction is 45.0% and the peak dislocation density is lower (~3.1×10¹³ m⁻²). The figure demonstrates that transient heating accelerates multi-stage dynamic recrystallization, with second-stage grains consuming the high-dislocation-density matrix to achieve near-complete recrystallization, supporting the conclusion that varying thermal paths produce distinct DRX kinetics and inhomogeneous dislocation density distributions compared to isothermal deformation.

Figure 27 presents simulated evolutions of average grain size (AGS) resulting from first-stage (DRXGs_I) and second-stage (DRXGs_II) dynamic recrystallization grains under varying deformation conditions using 3D cellular automaton (CA) models for low-alloy steel. The four panels compare transient changes in the Zener-Hollomon parameter (Z_P): (a) increasing strain rate from 0.01 to 0.1 s⁻¹ at 950 °C, (b) decreasing temperature from 1050 to 950 °C at 0.01 s⁻¹, (c) decreasing strain rate from 0.1 to 0.01 s⁻¹ at 950 °C, and (d) increasing temperature from 950 to 1050 °C at 0.01 s⁻¹. Each panel contains upper (ε_I = 0.2) and lower (ε_I = 0.4) subplots tracking Constant, First stage, and Second stage curves versus true strain. When Z_P is transiently increased, DRXGs_I remain significantly coarser than DRXGs_II, and their residual volume fraction rises with ε_I, elevating the final AGS. Conversely, when Z_P is decreased, DRXGs_I grow rapidly to sizes approaching constant-condition values before being refined by DRXGs_II, yielding final AGS close to constant conditions. These results quantitatively demonstrate how multi-stage recrystallization governs microstructural homogeneity under transient thermomechanical processing.
